data.title: S&P, Nasdaq set for record closes as stocks rally on tech boost, Russia-Ukraine hopes
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages jumped on Friday, amid a rally in tech stocks that boosted market sentiment, even as investors continued to weigh the impact of Donald Trump's tariffs.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.5%. Both the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) are on track for record closes, last +0.8% and +1%, respectively.

The Nasdaq's rise was supported by a number of big tech stocks rallied, including Tesla (TSLA) +2.3%, Palantir (PLTR) +2.2%, and Alphabet Class A (GOOGL) +2.4%.

Market participants also kept an eye on developments around Russia and Ukraine, as hopes of a truce has increased in recent days. Bloomberg News reported that Washington and Moscow were aiming to reach a deal that would halt the war while locking in Russia’s occupation of territory seized, citing people familiar with the matter. WTI crude oil futures (CL1:COM) initially fell on the report, seesawed and were last -0.4%.

“This week’s news once again highlights the rapid evolution and increasing versatility of AI, a general-purpose technology that is becoming smarter, faster, and more engaging. However, the impact on overall productivity depends on another crucial factor that is yet to attract sufficient attention: diffusion,” said Mohamed El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz.

“The degree to which this transformational innovation is adopted across various sectors in a relatively even manner is essential for sustainably enhancing economic growth potential,” El-Erian added.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 3 basis points to 4.29%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.76%.

Trump's reciprocal tariffs took effect at midnight on Thursday, and traders will keep a watch on the impact the trade policies have going ahead.

In other news, Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller is reportedly gaining momentum in the process to succeed Jerome Powell as chair of the central bank.

Friday's economic calendar is light, with St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem speaking at an event hosted by Mississippi Valley State University.

Wall Street's major averages on Thursday closed mixed, as positive sentiment sparked by U.S. President Donald Trump's chip tariff exemptions and progress on Russia-Ukraine de-escalation petered out.

data.title: Rackspace targets $70M–$80M in positive free cash flow for 2025 as AI and Private Cloud momentum accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rackspace Technology, Inc. (RXT) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Amar Maletira opened the call stating, "Results for the second quarter met our expectations across all key metrics. Revenue and operating profit exceeded the midpoint of our guided range, while EPS was within our guided range, marking our 12th consecutive quarter of meeting or exceeding guidance." Maletira highlighted strong sales pipeline generation, with bookings growing 2% sequentially and 16% year-over-year, primarily driven by Private Cloud.
  • Private Cloud bookings grew 24% sequentially and 42% year-over-year, with strong performance across healthcare, BFSI, and telecom. Maletira noted, "We also saw double-digit year-over-year bookings growth across both the Americas and EMEA." He emphasized a strategic expansion into mid-market and enterprise segments, and cited a new agreement with a leading U.S. healthcare provider and an expanded relationship with a large U.K. bank.
  • Public Cloud bookings grew 1% year-over-year, services bookings increased 6% sequentially, and revenue reached $417 million. Maletira stated, "We are also seeing success in increasing our footprint with existing relationships," referencing expansions with an aircraft leasing company and a cybersecurity firm. He introduced Rackspace CloudOps as a new managed service targeting mid-market customers.
  • AI initiatives made progress with over 80 wins and 235 pipeline opportunities. Maletira announced a strategic alliance with Sema4.ai and the launch of the Fair Model Context Protocol Enterprise Accelerator on AWS Marketplace, delivering "70% plus reduction in legacy application integration time."
  • CFO Mark Marino reported, "In the second quarter, total company GAAP revenue of $666 million was down 3% year-over-year and slightly up sequentially, beating our guidance, driven by solid performance across both business units." Marino added, "Non-GAAP operating profit was $27 million, exceeding the high end of our guidance and up 34% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • Marino provided guidance: "We expect third quarter GAAP revenue of $660 million to $674 million, flat sequentially and down 1% year-over-year at the midpoint. In Private Cloud, we expect revenue of $246 million to $254 million, flat sequentially and down 3% year-over-year at the midpoint. We expect Public Cloud revenue of $414 million to $420 million, flat sequentially at the midpoint. Total non-GAAP operating profit is expected to be $30 million to $32 million and non-GAAP loss per share is expected to be $0.04 to $0.06."
  • Marino projected, "In the second half of 2025, we expect strong free cash flow generation, positioning us to exit the year with $70 million to $80 million in positive free cash flow."

Financial Results

  • The company reported GAAP revenue of $666 million for the quarter, with Private Cloud revenue at $250 million and Public Cloud revenue at $417 million.
  • Non-GAAP operating profit reached $27 million. Non-GAAP loss per share was $0.06, at the lower end of guidance. Cash flow from operations was $8 million, and free cash flow was negative $12 million. Rackspace ended the quarter with $104 million in cash and $414 million in total liquidity.
  • Private Cloud non-GAAP gross margin was 36.8%, and non-GAAP segment operating margin was 24.6%. Public Cloud non-GAAP gross margin was 9.6%; segment operating margin was 3.9%.

Q&A

  • Kevin Damien McVeigh, UBS: Asked about guidance seasonality, free cash flow conversion, and services performance. Marino responded, "We did have some seasonality in the first half of the year related to some kind of onetime vendor prepayments, and those will not cycle in the second half. So that's driving a lot of our improvement as well as higher adjusted EBITDA and overall working capital performance."
  • McVeigh also inquired about services in Private and Public Cloud. Maletira explained, "We are starting to see broad-based strength across all those 3 services, mainly Professional Services," and noted, "About...we attach 70% of services to it. So for every dollar of infrastructure, we are attaching at least $0.70 of services."
  • Frank Garrett Louthan, Raymond James: Questioned mid-market investments and AI partnership benefits. Maletira stated, "Not much of investment -- incremental investments needed now from a go-to-market perspective...Most of the investments will be -- even the CapEx investments will be success-based."
  • On AI, Maletira described new wins in Private Cloud, such as, "We won a private AI infrastructure deal with a health care organization in the U.S.," and highlighted the implementation of agentic AI platforms with J.Crew and the Sema4.ai partnership.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was positive, with congratulatory remarks and focus on growth drivers and operational improvements.
  • Management tone remained confident in prepared remarks and Q&A, with phrases such as "we feel good about the bookings performance" and "pretty pleased with the performance."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts maintained or slightly increased optimism, especially around services and cash flow.
  • Management responses reflected strong confidence, supported by detailed data and forward-looking statements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q3 2025 is slightly higher at the midpoint compared to Q2. Management signaled continued stabilization in Private Cloud and a positive trajectory for Public Cloud services.
  • Bookings growth in Private Cloud accelerated from flat year-over-year in Q1 to 42% year-over-year in Q2. Public Cloud bookings decelerated from 16% to 1% year-over-year.
  • Management's confidence increased, emphasizing execution and improved deal mix. Analysts shifted from macro caution in Q1 to a greater focus on operational drivers in Q2.
  • Strategic focus moved further toward AI and mid-market expansion, building on Q1’s foundation of new product launches and regulated industry wins.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing revenue decline in Private Cloud, primarily due to customers rolling off older-generation offerings, partially offset by new bookings.
  • Seasonality and onetime prepayments affected first-half free cash flow, but are not expected to impact the second half.
  • No new macroeconomic concerns or regulatory issues were explicitly raised during the call.

Final Takeaway

Rackspace Technology highlighted another quarter of meeting or exceeding guidance with strong performance in Private Cloud bookings and accelerating AI initiatives. Management emphasized robust operational discipline, positive cash flow outlook, and expanding service offerings as key drivers. Guidance for the third quarter and the full-year free cash flow target reflect confidence in continued progress across strategic priorities, including AI partnerships and mid-market expansion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Greystone outlines $26.3M funding commitments and signals resilience amid muni bond market underperformance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Greystone Housing Impact Investors LP (GHI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Kenneth C. Rogozinski, CEO, reported no forbearance requests for multifamily mortgage revenue bonds, with all borrowers current on principal and interest payments as of June 30, 2025, and physical occupancy at 88.4% for the stabilized mortgage revenue bond portfolio.
  • The Vantage joint venture equity investments consist of interests in 5 properties, with 4 completed and 1 under evaluation. The Vantage at Helotes property was sold in May 2025, and leasing activity remains strong across initial lease-up phase properties.
  • Four joint venture equity investments with Freestone Development Group are progressing, including one completed project and others at various stages of leasing and construction. The Valage Senior Living Carson Valley project is now 49% occupied and pre-leased to 55%, aligning with original underwriting.
  • The Jessam at Hays Farm, a 318-unit property, is nearing construction completion and has begun leasing.
  • CFO Jesse A. Coury stated, "We reported a GAAP net loss of $7.1 million or $0.35 per unit, basic and diluted, and we reported cash available for distribution, or CAD, a non-GAAP measure, of positive $5.7 million or $0.25 per unit. Our second quarter GAAP net income was significantly impacted by provisions for credit losses and noncash unrealized losses on our interest rate derivatives, both of which are added back to net income or loss in our calculation of CAD."
  • Coury also explained, "We reported a provision for credit losses of $9.1 million during the second quarter, which is added back to net loss in calculating CAD, consistent with our historical treatment of credit loss allowances. The second quarter provision primarily relates to 3 nonprofit owner mortgage revenue bonds secured by properties in South Carolina."

Outlook

  • Management reported outstanding future funding commitments of $26.3 million for mortgage revenue bond, governmental issuer loan, and related investments as of June 30, to be funded over 12 months and expected to add to the income-producing asset base.
  • Coury noted, "These commitments will be funded over approximately 12 months and will add to our income-producing asset base."
  • Ongoing extension of secured lines of credit and new credit agreements are intended to increase borrowing capacity and operational flexibility.

Financial Results

  • GAAP net loss for the quarter was $7.1 million or ($0.35) per unit. Cash available for distribution (CAD) was reported as $5.7 million or $0.25 per unit.
  • A provision for credit losses of $9.1 million was primarily related to 3 nonprofit owner mortgage revenue bonds in South Carolina.
  • Book value per unit as of June 30 was $11.83, a decrease of $0.76 from March 31, attributed to the net loss and $0.30 per unit in distributions.
  • Unrestricted cash and cash equivalents were $47.5 million, with $86 million available on secured lines of credit.
  • Debt investments portfolio totaled $1.26 billion, comprising 85% of total assets, with 83 mortgage revenue bonds across 13 states.
  • During the quarter, $41 million was funded for mortgage revenue bond, governmental issuer loan, and related commitments, while $64 million was received from redemptions and paydowns.
  • The sale of the Vantage at Helotes property generated $17.1 million in proceeds, with $1.8 million in investment income and a $163,000 gain on sale.
  • Amended and new credit facilities increased borrowing capacity, including a new acquisition line of credit raised from $50 million to $80 million with an extended maturity.

Q&A

  • Matthew Erdner, JonesTrading: Asked for insights into muni bond underperformance. Rogozinski responded that elevated supply and gaps between new issuance and fund flows have pressured rates, while issues with large high-yield deals like the Brightline train system and American Dream mall have caused concern and "people are really sort of buckling down and focusing on the credits in their portfolio."
  • Erdner, JonesTrading: Inquired about guidance for pace of placements into the BlackRock JV. Rogozinski indicated that guidance on pace is not provided but historically, "pace pick[s] up in the second half of the year as states have a better handle on their available amount of private activity volume cap."
  • Christopher Muller, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked about the involvement of a new investor in the BlackRock JV. Rogozinski explained it was a mutual process and required extensive diligence and negotiation before securing the investment.
  • Muller, Citizens JMP Securities: Questioned additional capital advanced to Vantage properties and its impact on economic interest. Rogozinski stated it was due to longer holding periods and property tax payments, with no change to ownership interest.
  • Unidentified Private Investor: Asked about hedging strategy if rates decline and demand for multifamily housing. Rogozinski said the approach remains to avoid taking rate views and "expectation from our perspective is that we'll see lower funding costs, but we'll also be receiving lower payments from our counterparties on our interest rate swaps so that we're basically net neutral."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was probing but constructive, focusing on market underperformance, JV strategy, credit concerns, and operational details, with gratitude expressed for management's transparency.
  • Management's tone was steady and factual in prepared remarks, with Rogozinski providing detailed answers and reiterating risk mitigation and strategic flexibility, using language such as "I think," and "we expect," signaling a measured and slightly cautious outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone shifted from policy and tariff impact questions to concerns about credit losses, supply-demand dynamics, and JV capital deployment. Management maintained a consistent tone, addressing headwinds directly.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize precise funding commitments ($26.3 million) and the timeline for deployment over 12 months.
  • Strategic focus showed greater attention to credit loss provisions, with a significant $9.1 million provision in Q2 versus a reduction in Q1.
  • Analysts moved from policy and insurance cost discussions to a deeper focus on credit exposure, JV capital, and market underperformance.
  • Key metrics shifted from Q1's net income of $3.3 million to Q2's net loss of $7.1 million, with book value per unit declining further.
  • Management confidence in hedging and funding flexibility remained stable, though more time was spent explaining credit loss and market risks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified underperformance in property operations for three South Carolina nonprofit owner properties, leading to a $9.1 million provision for credit losses.
  • Unrealized losses on interest rate derivatives were reported at $2.1 million, attributed to market rate fluctuations.
  • Rogozinski highlighted ongoing challenges in the municipal bond market, including heavy supply, negative returns, and high-profile distressed deals impacting sentiment.
  • Management is actively engaging with property owners for operational improvements, refinancing, and sales options to maximize collateral value.

Final Takeaway

Greystone Housing Impact Investors LP closed the second quarter with a net loss, driven by credit loss provisions and unrealized derivative losses. Management underscored its steady liquidity position, expanded credit facilities, and $26.3 million in funding commitments intended to grow the income-producing asset base. Despite sector-wide muni bond underperformance and credit headwinds in select properties, the partnership continues to emphasize risk management, investment pipeline execution, and strategic joint ventures to navigate challenging market conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: PAR Technology says not bidding for Olo
data.description:
PAR Technology (NYSE:PAR) earlier said it's not bidding for Olo Inc. (NYSE:OLO) in response to a question on the company's Q2 earnings conference call. Shares of Olo fell 1.3%.

"While there's probably incredible synergies between our companies, we feel great where we are and our ability to take share," PAR CEO Savneet Singh said on the conference call  earlier on Friday.

The comments came after private equity firm Thoma Bravo agreed to purchase Olo  for $10.25 a share early last month. Bloomberg reported on April 30 that the restaurant software provider was evaluating a sale after seeing takeover interest.

The comments also follow after Olo (NYSE:OLO) disclosed in a filing that it received a  $13 a share cash/stock offer after it agreed to its planned sale to Thoma Bravo .An unidentified Party A offered the $13 a share unsolicited offer on July 3 for the SaaS platform for restaurants. 

Shares of PAR Technology (NYSE:PAR) slumped 19% after reporting Q2 results on Friday.

data.title: Verastem climbs on Q2 beat as new ovarian cancer drug boosts topline
data.description:
Verastem (NASDAQ:VSTM) shares added ~22% on Friday after the oncology-focused biotech reported better-than-expected financials for Q2 2025 as its newly approved ovarian cancer therapy, Avmapki Fakzynja Co-pack, was off to a strong commercial launch.

Needham, Massachusetts-based Verastem (NASDAQ:VSTM) rolled out Avmapki Fakzynja Co-pack a week after the FDA approved the oral therapy as a late-line option for KRAS-mutated recurrent low-grade serous ovarian cancer in May.

“We are off to a strong start with the launch of this innovative combination therapy,” CEO Dan Paterson remarked.

According to Verastem (NASDAQ:VSTM), Avmapki Fakzynja Co-pack generated $2.1M of net product revenue during the first six weeks of its launch, expanding the company’s Q1 topline to $2.1M, which topped the consensus by $1.0M.

However, VSTM’s non-GAAP net loss grew ~151% YoY to $25.9M, still beating the consensus estimates by $0.11 per share, while its SG&A expenses more than doubled to $20.7M, mainly due to costs associated with the product launch and other expenditures.

“Our focus for the second half of the year is to continue to build on the positive launch momentum,” CEO Paterson added.

data.title: Gladstone Land signals $17M shift to participation rents while maintaining 6.1% yield
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Gladstone Land Corporation (LAND) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO David John Gladstone reported that the company managed about 103,000 acres across 150 farms with over 55,000 acre-feet of water assets, noting, "We continue to be cautious and have made no new investments because interest rates and the expenses of running these farms are so different now than they were when we first started." He emphasized a significant change in lease structure, stating, "We've decided to adjust the lease structure on 6 properties...we have moved from being a leaser and more of an operator or a grower of sorts because we're taking some of our payment for the lease in part of the crop that is being grown."
  • Gladstone highlighted the transition of eight farms to crop share or direct operation arrangements, supported by robust crop insurance coverage. He mentioned the sale of two Florida farms under contract and described ongoing efforts to revert modified leases back to fixed base rent where possible.
  • Bill Ryman, discussing the operated properties, explained, "Three of these properties are wine grape vineyards...the remaining 5 properties consist of 2 pistachio orchards, 2 almond orchards and a large property that has both," noting strong crop conditions and anticipation of above-average yields.
  • CFO Lewis Parrish stated, "For the second quarter, we recorded a net loss of about $7.9 million and a net loss to common shareholders of $13.9 million or $0.38 per share. Adjusted FFO was negative $3.4 million or $0.10 per share compared to a positive $3.7 million or $0.10 per share in the same quarter last year." Parrish attributed the year-over-year decline in AFFO to lease structure changes, tenant vacancies, and lost revenue from sales.
  • Parrish added, "Fixed base cash rents were down by about $6.8 million from the prior year quarter due to...vacancies we continue to work through and the structural changes made to certain leases."

Outlook

  • The company reported that lease changes are expected to reduce fixed base rents by about $17 million for fiscal year 2025 compared to 2024, with the majority of resulting crop share proceeds to be recognized as participation rent in the fourth quarter of 2025 and the remaining portion in the second half of 2026.
  • Gladstone stated, "We have changed when we can recognize income, and we won't recognize -- we recognize very little in the second quarter. Hopefully, in the third and fourth quarter, certainly the fourth quarter as we sell a lot of our crops, we'll be back in the game of lots of profits."
  • Parrish noted, "We're maintaining the dividend at its current level for now, and we'll reevaluate it in the coming months as we gain more clarity on the 2025 harvest results."

Financial Results

  • The company posted a net loss of $7.9 million and a net loss to common shareholders of $13.9 million or $0.38 per share for Q2 2025. Adjusted FFO was negative $3.4 million or $0.10 per share.
  • Dividends declared per common share were $0.14 in both Q2 2025 and the prior year quarter, and the current annualized yield stands at 6.1% based on a $9.14 share price.
  • Core operating expenses decreased by about $200,000 this quarter, and cash operating expenses fell by $135,000, offset by higher property operating expenses mainly due to costs to protect water rights and increased property taxes from farm vacancies.
  • The company holds over $150 million in available capital and nearly $170 million in unpledged properties.

Q&A

  • Gaurav Mehta, Alliance Global Partners, asked about the $17 million shift to participation rents and its timing. Parrish responded, "It's difficult for us to say right now because we don't know what the total number is going to be...we're penciling probably about 60% to 65% this year and the rest would be a little bit throughout the year, but most of it would be probably in Q4 of next year."
  • Mehta asked if leases would automatically revert to fixed rents. Gladstone clarified, "They never automatically convert to fixed, and so we'll have to negotiate that again when the time frame comes up..."
  • Mehta inquired about the Series D preferred stock redemption. Parrish explained, "Our options are we could pay it off with potentially with proceeds from property sales, the line of credit...We're still assessing things internally, cash availability, line of credit, seeing what makes sense to do come January."
  • Mehta asked about crop type trends. Bill Ryman noted, "Not really. You have the ups and downs in some of the annual row crops, but those are just kind of the normal whims of the market...The markets that are important to us are the permanent crops because even in standard leases, a component of those leases is crop share."
  • Steven Dumanski, Janney Montgomery Scott, questioned disposition opportunities and water asset changes. Gladstone said, "Certainly, there are buyers out there, but they're all looking at very discounted prices for farms..."
  • Dumanski asked about the quarter-over-quarter decrease in acre-feet of water. Parrish clarified, "That was just because we used a small amount. Now we record water credit recognition in the quarter in arrears...it was just some tenant transition on one property."
  • John Massocca, B. Riley Securities, focused on Sigma's regulatory impact and water security. Gladstone and Ryman described ongoing regulatory changes and proactive water storage strategies, with Ryman stating, "Our focus immediately went to a long-term viewpoint on it. That's why the investment in delivery infrastructure, identifying groundwater basins where we can store water."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly negative, pressing for clarity on lease revenue timing, cash management, and regulatory impacts.
  • Management maintained a cautiously optimistic tone in prepared remarks but showed signs of hesitation and caution in the Q&A, often referencing uncertainty in crop outcomes and lease renegotiations. Gladstone said, "Hopefully, in the third and fourth quarter, certainly the fourth quarter as we sell a lot of our crops, we'll be back in the game of lots of profits."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shows more urgency and direct acknowledgement of near-term challenges, while analysts continue to probe for specifics on financial visibility and strategic risk.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company maintained its strategic focus on shifting lease structures from fixed rent to participation/crop share, but in Q2, it provided more detail on the operational transition and its financial impact.
  • Guidance language is more explicit about the timing and scale of the $17 million fixed rent reduction, with earnings now expected to be heavily weighted to Q4.
  • Both quarters highlighted uncertainty due to market and regulatory factors affecting crop yields and pricing, but Q2 remarks reflect a heightened sense of transition risk and the need to renegotiate leases or potentially sell assets.
  • Analyst concerns remained focused on lease income timing, liquidity, and regulatory issues, with management displaying more transparency about operational challenges and liquidity strategy in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted exposure to volatile crop prices, tenant vacancies, and uncertainty regarding lease renewals and participation rent outcomes.
  • Regulatory risk remains significant, particularly regarding water rights and Sigma-related changes in California, with ongoing legal actions and infrastructure investments aimed at mitigation.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the timing and reliability of participation rent, asset dispositions, and the impact of water regulation on asset value.

Final Takeaway

Gladstone Land Corporation underscored a pivotal transition in its revenue model, shifting a substantial portion of income from fixed base rents to participation rents tied to crop outcomes, with $17 million in fixed rent reductions for 2025 expected to be weighted toward the fourth quarter. While the company maintains solid liquidity, a strong focus on water security, and a consistent dividend, near-term visibility is limited as the outcome of crop yields and lease renegotiations will determine profitability. Management emphasized its proactive approach to mitigating operational and regulatory risks, while signaling that financial results and leasing structures may remain dynamic through year-end as market and regulatory conditions evolve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NVR to buy back up to $750M of common stock
data.description:
NVR (NYSE:NVR) has been authorized by the board to repurchase up to $750M of its outstanding common stock, the homebuilder said on Friday.

The repurchase authorization does not have an expiration date.

At Aug. 7, 2025, NVR (NYSE:NVR) had 2.87M common shares outstanding.

NVR inched up 0.2% in midafternoon trading.

data.title: Sensus Healthcare outlines global expansion and regulatory milestones amid reimbursement uncertainty
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sensus Healthcare (SRTS) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Joseph C. Sardano, CEO, reported that domestic sales momentum was disrupted mid-quarter due to a proposed local coverage determination (LCD) that would limit reimbursement for ultrasound with the SRT-100 Vision systems. Sardano noted, “Up to that point in Q2, we were on track to surpass expectations, yet we found our market at a pause until there's clarification, which we expect soon.” He highlighted that significant investments are being made to lobby CMS for support of ultrasound usage, emphasizing, “We are in lockstep with our major customer as we work together to help the powers of B understand the importance of high-frequency ultrasound in treating non-melanoma skin cancer.” Sardano also pointed to a proposed Medicare physician fee schedule that could “significantly increase reimbursement for the delivery of a code of SRT.”

  • The company delivered 19 SRT systems in Q2, including 4 to China. Sardano described growing international demand and referenced the appointment of Radiation Oncology Systems (ROS) as the new primary U.S. hospital-based oncology distributor, stating, “We expect to see results this year.”

  • Michael J. Sardano, President, detailed execution on the three-pronged strategy: “increase patient awareness for SRT, grow internationally and advance our pipeline.” He spotlighted the MDSAP regulatory certification as a milestone, now opening “immediate access to the markets of Brazil, Canada, Japan and Australia.”

  • CFO Javier Rampolla reported, “Revenues for the second quarter of 2025 were $7.3 million compared with $9.2 million for the second quarter of 2024.” He added, “Net loss for the second quarter of 2025 was $1 million or $0.06 per share compared with net income of $1.6 million or $0.10 per diluted share for the second quarter of 2024.”

Outlook

  • Management expects clarification on the LCD issue in the near term and is optimistic that lobbying efforts will prevent its implementation. Michael J. Sardano indicated, “We don't believe that [the LCD] is going to come through due to our lobbying efforts and a few other entities out there lobbying.”

  • The new Medicare physician fee schedule, if finalized, could “make it like you just said, about a 300-plus percent increase in the delivery code for SRT, which would be pretty amazing.”

  • International expansion is expected to accelerate following MDSAP certification, with Michael J. Sardano stating, “We are actively working with potential commercial partners that serve those countries and laying the foundation for additional revenue contributions potentially as early as later this year.”

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q2 2025 was reported as $7.3 million, with a gross profit of $2.9 million and a gross margin of 39.7%. Selling and marketing expenses increased to $1.4 million, and R&D expense rose to $1.5 million, primarily due to TDI development and reimbursement lobbying. Net loss for the quarter was ($1.0 million), or ($0.06) per share. The company ended the quarter with $22.2 million in cash and no debt.

  • Year-to-date revenues were $15.7 million against $19.9 million for the first half of 2024. The decline was attributed to fewer system sales to a large customer, partially offset by growth in recurring revenue from Fair Deal Agreements.

Q&A

  • Benjamin Charles Haynor, Lake Street Capital, asked about the relationship between the LCD and the new CMS codes. Michael J. Sardano responded that the LCD and physician fee schedule are “2 separate items,” and expressed confidence that the LCD would not take effect, while the fee schedule could have a “300-plus percent increase in the delivery code for SRT.”

  • Haynor queried whether the LCD proposal caused a slowdown in treatment volumes. Michael J. Sardano explained, “everyone kind of just -- what happened is it just a pause because there was no clear path right there as far as whether it was going to happen or not.”

  • Anthony V. Vendetti, Maxim Group, asked if a large customer paused purchases and whether this would impact Q3 sales. CEO Sardano confirmed, “anytime anything in health care is unclear, everybody waits to wait until they have the final verdict as to what's going on,” but stressed, “Our customer is committed to IGSRT from Sensus Healthcare.”

  • Vendetti also questioned international sales prospects, to which CEO Sardano said, “China will continue to order, but we're looking forward to other orders from other parts of the world.”

  • Eduardo Rafael Martinez-Montes, H.C. Wainwright, inquired about the timeline for ROS and international expansion. Michael J. Sardano noted that some international partnerships “are going to be immediate,” highlighting upcoming travel and meetings in Japan and Taiwan.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone reflected concern and uncertainty, particularly around the LCD and its potential impact on sales, with questions focused on timing, customer response, and international offsets.

  • Management was more confident and reassuring in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently emphasizing ongoing lobbying and strong customer relationships. Phrases such as “we don't believe that it's going to come through due to our lobbying efforts” and “Our customer is committed” reflected optimism, though some defensive and explanatory responses were notable.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management were more cautious, with increased attention to external regulatory risks and their near-term sales implications.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from Q1’s expectation of sequential profitability and revenue growth to a more cautious outlook in Q2, as reimbursement uncertainty became a dominant theme.

  • Strategic focus broadened from U.S.-driven FDA and Fair Deal Agreement expansion to a stronger emphasis on international growth and regulatory milestones, such as the MDSAP certification.

  • Analysts’ questions pivoted from detailed Fair Deal Agreement ramp-up in Q1 to regulatory and macro reimbursement issues in Q2.

  • Key financial metrics declined sequentially, with Q2 revenue and profit down compared to Q1, and management commentary reflecting greater caution.

Risks and Concerns

  • The primary challenge discussed was the proposed LCD, which has caused a pause in purchasing decisions by key customers. Management is investing in lobbying efforts and expects a resolution before year end.

  • Potential sales disruption in Q3 is possible if reimbursement clarification is delayed. Management noted the risk of an “outsized or significant impact in sales for the quarter ending September 30.”

  • International market expansion may help offset U.S. pauses, but order timing remains variable.

  • The company faces higher operating expenses from increased investments in R&D and lobbying, impacting near-term profitability.

Final Takeaway

Sensus Healthcare management conveyed that while temporary regulatory uncertainties have stalled domestic sales momentum, ongoing lobbying and strong customer relationships provide a path to recovery. The company is leveraging recent international regulatory achievements and new commercial partnerships to drive future growth, expecting clarity on reimbursement to unlock both U.S. and global market opportunities as the year progresses.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Granite Ridge raises 2025 production guidance by 10% with $400M–$420M capex plan as new CEO advances partnership strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Granite Ridge Resources (GRNT) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Granite Ridge Resources announced a leadership transition with Tyler S. Farquharson appointed as the new CEO following Luke Brandenberg’s departure. Farquharson acknowledged, “Luke played a pivotal role in shaping Granite Ridge into the successful business it is today. And on behalf of the team, I want to express our gratitude for his significant contributions. We wish him all the best in his next chapter.”
  • Farquharson reported, “Our quarterly results continue to validate our business model with production and cash flow once again exceeding expectations.” He detailed the company’s Q2 performance: “We turned 4.9 net wells to sales and increased production by 37% year-over-year to 31,576 barrels of oil equivalent per day, driven by a 46% rise in oil production and a 28% rise in natural gas production.”
  • Granite Ridge raised its full-year production guidance by 10% at the midpoint to between 31,000 and 33,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day and increased capital expenditure guidance to an all-in range of $400 million to $420 million, primarily due to new unbudgeted acquisitions.
  • The company’s ongoing strategy is to expand its operated partnership and non-op investment strategies, particularly in the Permian and Appalachian basins. Farquharson stated, “We have identified nearly $60 million of new inventory acquisitions, $40 million of which are located in the Permian Basin for our operator partners and $20 million of which are driven by organic acreage leasing in the Utica Shale.”
  • Farquharson emphasized, “Our vision is to become the leading public investment platform for energy development, and we will continue to invest alongside proven high-quality operating teams to capitalize on undervalued opportunity.”
  • Kimberly A. Weimer, Interim CFO & Chief Accounting Officer, reported, “In Q2, we generated total oil and gas sales revenue of $109.2 million, an increase of 20% compared to Q2 2024. This growth was driven by a 37% increase in production to 31,576 BOE per day.” She added, “Net income for the quarter was $25.1 million or $0.19 per share… Operating cash flow before working capital changes was $69.5 million.”

Outlook

  • The company raised its full-year production guidance to 31,000–33,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day and capital expenditure guidance to $400 million to $420 million.
  • For Q3, management expects “production to modestly grow above Q2 levels with further growth in Q4 as new wells come online.”
  • Granite Ridge anticipates deploying approximately $120 million in acquisition capital for 2025, adding 74 net locations, with 80% targeting the Permian Basin.
  • The company will now provide separate guidance for development and acquisition capital expenditures.

Financial Results

  • Granite Ridge reported total oil and gas sales revenue of $109.2 million for Q2 2025, a 20% increase from Q2 2024.
  • Oil revenues rose by $12 million with a 46% jump in oil production to 16,009 barrels per day, while realized prices declined from $77.84 to $61.41 per barrel.
  • Natural gas revenues increased by $6.6 million driven by a 28% rise in production and a 17% improvement in realized prices.
  • Operating cash flow before working capital changes was $69.5 million.
  • Lease operating expenses rose to $20.1 million or $7 per BOE, reflecting higher service and saltwater disposal costs.
  • General and administrative expenses increased to $8.5 million, including $1.7 million in severance related to the leadership transition and $1.1 million tied to capital markets activities.
  • Development CapEx for the first half of 2025 totaled $148.6 million while acquisition CapEx was $44.4 million. The company’s leverage ratio stands at 0.8x net debt to adjusted EBITDA after increasing long-term debt to $275 million.

Q&A

  • John Phillips Little Johnston, Capital One: Asked about the drivers behind the higher oil mix in the second half of 2025. CEO Farquharson responded that the oil cut is expected to be “more like 52%,” with growth predominantly from the Permian, which has an oilier mix.
  • Johnston inquired about the Board’s willingness to add to net debt. Farquharson indicated comfort operating in a 1 to 1.25x leverage band, currently at 0.8x, and stated, “We think you’ll continue to see us outspend cash flow until we get to where we’re comfortable with leverage.”
  • Unidentified Analyst, Texas Capital: Asked what gives confidence to pursue acquisitions. Farquharson cited reduced private equity capital in the sector and increased A&D activity, saying, “This is the most constructive environment that we’ve seen on the A&D front in quite some time.”
  • Michael Scialla, Stephens: Questioned capex and rig count for 2026. Farquharson said, “I could see a scenario definitely where we’re with a fourth rig… I do expect that we’d be running at least 1 rig on one of those 2 new partners.” He expects 2026 capex to be similar to or higher than 2025 if conditions persist.
  • Noah Hungness, Bank of America: Asked about growth trajectory. Farquharson stated, “Production growth isn’t a target… I would expect quite a bit of growth into 2026… might not be as strong as this year, but would be mid-teens type growth given what we’re buying now.”
  • Hungness also asked about credit market plans. Farquharson explained, “We increased the RBL by $50 million in the spring… I’d expect us to be able to get a pretty healthy increase again in the fall… we’re also looking at options that would help us term out some of that RBL balance.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was generally constructive but pressed management on leverage, growth sustainability, and capex discipline, showing a slightly positive to neutral sentiment overall.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident, with Farquharson stating, “Our strategy is proven, our assets are exceptional, and our team is best in class.” In Q&A, he remained composed and direct, particularly about leverage and acquisition strategy.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more assertive and optimistic on growth and acquisition opportunities; analysts remained focused on capital discipline and sustainability, similar to the prior quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most notable change was the CEO transition from Luke Brandenberg to Tyler S. Farquharson.
  • Guidance for production and capital expenditures has increased, with the company now targeting higher production and more acquisition-driven growth compared to the previous quarter’s more measured approach.
  • Management now expects to outspend cash flow longer, citing a robust acquisition environment, whereas in Q1 the emphasis was on remaining cash flow neutral (excluding dividends).
  • Analysts’ focus shifted more toward leverage and acquisition strategy as opposed to the previous call’s emphasis on cost structure and production mix.
  • Management’s confidence level is higher, underscored by more aggressive capital deployment and a shift toward expanding operator partnerships.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged continued oil and gas price volatility and increased service and saltwater disposal costs.
  • The company’s higher capex and acquisition spend—outspending cash flow—raises leverage risk, though management stated comfort in a 1 to 1.25x leverage range.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of growth and the risk of accumulating debt as acquisition activity accelerates.
  • Nonrecurring G&A expenses tied to leadership transition and capital markets activity temporarily elevated costs.

Final Takeaway

Granite Ridge Resources enters the second half of 2025 with new leadership, a raised production and capital spending outlook, and a clear focus on leveraging operator partnerships in the Permian and Appalachia. The company’s disciplined approach to acquisitions and capital allocation supports robust production growth, while management expresses strong confidence in navigating ongoing market volatility and delivering long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Centerra Gold cut at BMO on operational volatility, cost increases
data.description:
Centerra Gold (NYSE:CGAU) -5.5% in Friday's trading as BMO Capital downgraded shares to Market Perform from Outperform with a C$10 price target, down from C$14, following operational volatility at Mt. Milligan and cost increases at Oksut.

BMO analyst Raj Ray said he is looking for more visibility on operational improvements, execution on projects, and capital allocation, noting Centerra (NYSE:CGAU) shares have materially lagged peers during the past 12 months despite strong shareholder capital returns, as continued operational 
volatility has been an issue alongside the market's expectations for a strategic partner for the molybdenum project execution which has been elusive.

Also, Ray believes the decision to proceed with the Goldfield project is unlikely to excite investors given that the scale of the project and exploration potential have been subpar so far, while significantly lowering the 2025-27 free cash flow profile.

The analyst also noted that Mt. Milligan's operational weakness has caused a revision of Centerra's (CGAU) FY 2025 production guidance to 250K-290K oz from 270K-310K oz prior and all-in sustaining cost outlook is now US$1,650-$1,750/oz from US$1,400-$1,500/oz previously.
